
```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(reticulate)
library(bookdown)
library(MASS)
library(ggplot2)
library(class)

set.seed(20021122)
#use_python("C:\\Users\\User\\AppData\\Local\\Programs\\Python\\Python313\\python.exe")

color <- function(x, color) {
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    sprintf("\\textcolor{%s}{%s}", color, x)
  } else if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
    sprintf("<span style='color: %s;'>%s</span>", color,
      x)
  } else x
}
```

# Overview of Supervised Learning

## Variable Types and Terminologies

Symbols and variable conventions follow the rules outlined below by the textbook: $\newline$ Input variable is denoted by the symbol $X$. If $X$ is a vector, then its components is accessed by subscripts $X_j$. Quantitative outputs are denoted by $Y$, and qualitative outputs by $G$. Observed values are written in lowercase; for example the $i$th observed value of $X$ is written as $x_i$ (where $x_i$ is a scalar or vector). Matrices are represented by bold uppercase letters; for example, a set of $N$ input $p$-vectors $x_i$, $i = 1, ... , N$ is represented by the $N \times p$ matrix $\textbf{X}$. In general, vectors will not be bold, except when they have $N$ components; this convention distinguishes a $p$-vector of inputs $x_i$ for the $i$th observations from the $N$-vector $\textbf{x}_j$ consisting of all the observations on variable $X_j$. Since all vectors are assumed to be column vectors, the $i$th row of $\textbf{X}$ is $x_i^{T}$, the vector transpose of $x_i$.

For most learning tasks, we can understand it as such: given an input vector $X$, we want to make a good prediction of the output $Y$, denoted as $\hat{Y}$. If $Y\in\mathbb{R}$ then $\hat{Y}$ too; likewise for categorical outputs, $\hat{G}$ should take values in the same set $\mathcal{G}$ associated with $G$.

For accurate prediction, data is necessary. We denote training data as a set of measurements $(x_i,y_i)$ or $(x_i,g_i)$, $i = 1,...,N$.

## Prediction Approaches: Least Squares and Nearest Neighbors

Two prediction methods are explored here: The linear model fit by least squares and the $k$-nearest neighbor prediction rule.

### Linear Models and Least Squares

Given a vector of inputs $X^{T} = (X_1, X_2, ..., X_n)$, we predict the output $Y$ via the model: 

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta_0} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_j\hat{\beta}_j
(\#eq:leq)
\end{equation}
$$

where $\hat{\beta_0}$ is the intercept. Equivalently, we can write the linear model in vector form as an inner product: 

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat{Y} = X^{T}\hat{\beta}
(\#eq:leqvec)
\end{equation}
$$

where $X = \begin{pmatrix}1\\x_1\\\vdots\\x_n\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbb{R^{n+1}}$ and $\hat{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix}\hat{\beta}_0\\\hat{\beta}_1\\\vdots\\\hat{\beta}_n\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$, such that $\hat{Y} = \sum_{i=0}^{n}\hat{\beta}_i x_i$. 

If $\textbf{X}$ is instead a feature matrix with $p$ features and $m$ samples such that $\textbf{X}\in\mathbb{R^{m\times p}}$, where $p=n+1$, we can represent $\textbf{X}^{T}$ as a $p$-dimensional row vector $\begin{bmatrix} X_0 \; X_1 \; X_2 \; ... \; X_n \end{bmatrix}$, where each $X_j$ is a column vector in $\mathbb{R^m}$. For example, $X_1^T = [x_1^{(1)} \; x_1^{(2)} \; \ldots \; x_1^{(m)}]$, $X_0$ is a constant vector of $1$ and suppose that $\hat{Y}$ is a $k$-dimensional row vector instead of a scalar, then by equation \@ref(eq:leq), $\beta$ must necessarily be a $p \times K$ matrix of coefficients.

<details>
<summary>Remark on linear model as a hyperplane.</summary>
:::{.remark}
In the $(p+1)$-dimensional input-output space, the tuple $(X, \hat{Y})$ represents a hyperplane. This follows directly from the linear structure of equation \@ref(eq:leqvec):

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\hat{Y} &= X^{T}\beta \\
        &= \sum_{i=0}^{n} \beta_i x_i \\
        &= \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^n \beta_i x_i
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Swapping the LHS with the RHS term:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
-\beta_0 &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i x_i - \hat{Y}\\
         &= \sum_{i=1}^n \beta_i x_i + (-1)\hat{Y}\\
         &= (\beta_1 \; \beta_2 \; ... \beta_n)\;\cdot\;(x_1 \; x_2 \; ... \; \hat{Y})^T \\
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

A hyperplane is defined as $\textbf{w}^{T}\textbf{z}= b$. In our case, by letting $\textbf{w} = (\beta_1 \; \beta_2 \; ... \beta_n)^T$ and $\textbf{z}=(x_1 \; x_2 \;...\;\hat{Y})^T$, we obtain $\textbf{w}^{T}\textbf{z} = -\beta_0$ (our intercept $\beta_0$ translates the hyperplane away from the origin).
:::
</details>

### Residual Sum of Squares

To fit our training data to the linear model, we use the method of least squares. We pick the coefficients $\beta$ that minimize the residual sum of square:

$$
\begin{equation}
RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - x_{i}^{T}\beta)^2
(\#eq:RSS)
\end{equation}
$$

$RSS(\beta)$ is a quadratic function of the parameters, hence its minimum always exist but may not be unique. 

<details>
<summary>Remark on the existence and non-uniqueness of a minimum for RSS</summary>
:::{.remark}
We first prove that a minimum always exists for the RSS by establishing that RSS is a convex function with at least one critical point, which guarantees a global minimum.

To analyze the structure of RSS, we expand the quadratic form:

$$
\begin{equation}
RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - x_{i}^{T}\beta)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i^2 + (x_{i}^{T}\beta)^2 - 2y_ix_i^{T}\beta)
\end{equation}
$$

By linearity of summation and the dot product identities $\sum_{i=1}^N x_ix_i^T= \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ and $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^N x_iy_i$, this becomes:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
RSS(\beta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N}\beta^Tx_ix_i^T\beta - \sum_{i=1}^{N}2y_ix_i^{T}\beta\\
           &= ||\textbf{y}||^2 +\beta^T\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta - 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}\beta
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

To establish convexity for RSS, we show its Hessian matrix $\nabla^2 RSS(\beta) = 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is positive semidefinite as RSS is twice differentiable. A matrix M is positive semidefinite if $x^TMx \geq 0$ for all $x$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$. As:

$$
\begin{equation}
v^T\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}v = (\textbf{X}v)^T\textbf{X}v = ||\textbf{X}v||^2\geq0
\end{equation}
$$

, we showed that RSS is convex.

Next, we need to establish that a critical point exists and this critical point must necessarily be a minimum. To find a critical point, we compute the gradient[^1]:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\nabla_{\beta}RSS(\beta) &= -2\textbf{X}^T(\textbf{y}-\textbf{X}\beta) \\
                         &= -2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} + 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Thus, a critical point must satisfy the normal equation $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta$. The problem of determining the existence of a critical point can now be reduced to a simple problem of determining that the normal equation is consistent:

A linear system $Ax = b$ has a solution if and only if $b \in \text{Col}(A)$. For the normal equations $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}$, we need $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \in \text{col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$. Since $\text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}) = \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T)$[^2] and $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}$ is, by definition, a linear combination of the columns of $\textbf{X}^T$, we have $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \in \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T) = \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$. Since a consistent system has at least one solution, RSS always has at least one critical point.

Lastly, we show that for convex functions, any critical point is necessarily a global minimum. Let $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ be convex and differentiable at $x^*$ for any $x\in\mathbb{R}^n$, and $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$, then $x^*$ is a global minimum. By the first-order characterization of convexity[^3]:

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) \geq f(x^*) + \nabla f^T(x^*)(x-x^*) =  f(x^*) + 0(x-x^*) = f(x^*)
\end{equation}
$$

As $f(x) \geq f(x^*)$ for any $x,x^*\in dom(f)$, $f(x^*)$ is a global minimum. Therefore a minimum always exists for RSS.

However, this minimum is not necessarily unique. The uniqueness depends entirely on whether $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible, leading to two cases:

Case 1: $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible:

If the matrix $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible, then:

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat\beta = (\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})^{-1}\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}
\end{equation}
$$ 

, and $\hat{\beta}$ is a unique solution.

Case 2: $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is singular:

When $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is singular, $\text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$ is nontrivial. If $\beta_0$ is a solution to the normal equation and $\beta^*\in\text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$, such that $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta^* = 0$, then:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}(\beta_0 + \beta^*) = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta_0 + \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta^* = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} + 0 = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Hence $\beta_0 + \beta^*$ is itself a solution and it forms the solution set $\{\beta_0 + \beta^*|\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \wedge \beta^*\in \text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})\}$. Since $\text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$ is a nontrivial linear subspace and has infinitely many elements[^4], the solution set forms an affine subspace with infinitely many elements.

Hence, while the minimum always exists, it is only unique when $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible.
:::
</details>
<br>
As shown previously, $\text{RSS}(\beta)$ can be written in the matrix notation:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{RSS}(\beta) &= \textbf{y}^T\textbf{y} +\beta^T\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta - 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}\beta\\
                  &= (\textbf{y}-\textbf{X}^T\beta)^T(y-\textbf{X}^T\beta)
\end{split}
(\#eq:RSSmatrix)
\end{equation}
$$

where $\textbf{X}$ is an $N \times p$ matrix with each row an input vector, ,and $\textbf{y}$ is an $N$-vector of the outputs in the training set. Differentiating w.r.t $\beta$ we get the normal equations:

$$
\begin{equation}
\textbf{X}^T(\textbf{y} - \textbf{X}\beta) = 0
(\#eq:normeq)
\end{equation}
$$
If $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is nonsingular, then we get the unique solution:

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat\beta = (\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})^{-1}\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}
\end{equation}
(\#eq:normsol)
$$ 

and the fitted value at the $i$th input $x_i$ is $\hat{y}_i = \hat{y}_i(x_i) = x^{T}_i\hat{\beta}$. At an arbitrary input $x_0$, the prediction is $\hat{y}_i(x_0) = x^{T}_0\hat{\beta}$. The entire fitted surface is characterized by the $p$ parameters $\hat{\beta}$.[^5] It seems that we may not need a very large data set to fit such a model. 

### Linear Model Simulation 

We simulate a data set with an output class variable $G$ with the values `r color("BLUE", "blue")` or `r color("ORANGE", "orange")`. There are 100 points in each of the two classes. We fit a linear regression model to these values, with the response $Y$ coded as $0$ for `r color("BLUE", "blue")` and $1$ for `r color("ORANGE", "orange")`. The fitted values $\hat{Y}$ are converted to a fitted class variable $\hat{G}$ according to the rule

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat{G} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{ORANGE} & \hat{Y} > 0.5\\
\text{BLUE} & \hat{Y} \leq 0.5
\end{cases}
(\#eq:classlabel)
\end{equation}
$$

There are multiple scenarios where our constructed data can come from, but we outline here specifically two types:

$\textbf{Scenario 1}$: The training data in each class were generated from bivariate Gaussian distributions with uncorrelated components and different means. 

In other words, our data is generated from each class $j$ with fixed mean $\mu^{(j)}$ and covariance $\Sigma^{(j)}$, and each observation $i$ is distributed with $X_i|Y=j \sim N(\mu^{(j)}, \Sigma^{(j)})$. Therefore, $P(X|Y=j) = \phi(x;\mu^{(j)},\Sigma^{(j)})$.

$\textbf{Scenario 2}$: The training data in each class came from a mixture of 10 low-variance Gaussian distributions, with individual means themselves distributed as Gaussian. 

Essentially, we have $10$ random cluster locations $\mu_k^{(j)}$ per class, each distributed around a class specific mean $\mu^{(j)}$ with covariance $\Sigma^{(j)}$. Then for each cluster location, we generate an observation $X_i$ where $X_i|\text{Location}=k,Y=j \sim N(\mu_k^{(j)}, \Sigma)$ where $\Sigma$ is the variance of the mixture distribution. Therefore, $P(X|Y=j) = \sum_{k=1}^{10}\pi_k\phi(x;\mu_k^{(j)},\Sigma)$, where $\pi_k$ is the weight of the cluster (How likely/prominent this cluster is).

We first demonstrate the method of how our simulated data set is generated: 

```{r, warning=FALSE}
# Generate data set of n=20 from two bivariate normal distribution
d1 <- mvrnorm(n=10, c(1,0), diag(2)) #BLUE
d2 <- mvrnorm(n=10, c(0,1), diag(2)) #ORANGE

gen_obs <- function(mean, nobs){
  obs <- c()
  for (i in 1:nobs){
    row_mean <- mean[sample(1:10, 1), ]
    newobs <- mvrnorm(n=1, row_mean, diag(1/5, 2, 2))
    obs <- rbind(obs, unname(newobs))
  }
  
  colnames(obs) <- c("x1", "x2")
  return(obs)
}

d1_obs <- gen_obs(d1, 100)
d2_obs <- gen_obs(d2, 100)
Y <- rep(c(0,1), each=100) # 0=BLUE, 1=ORANGE
test_mat <- as.data.frame(cbind(Y, rbind(d1_obs, d2_obs)))
test_mat$Y <- factor(test_mat$Y, labels = c("Blue", "Orange"))

# Generate points grid 
grid_size <- 100
x1_range <- range(test_mat$x1)
x2_range <- range(test_mat$x2)
grid_x1 <- seq(x1_range[1] - 1, x1_range[2] + 1, length.out = grid_size)
grid_x2 <- seq(x2_range[1] - 1, x2_range[2] + 1, length.out = grid_size)
grid_data <- expand.grid(x1 = grid_x1, x2 = grid_x2) 
```

We generated $10$ centers $m_k^{(1)} \sim N((1,0)^T, I)$ for `r color("ORANGE", "orange")` class and $10$ centers $m_k^{(0)} \sim N((1,0)^T, I)$ for `r color("BLUE", "blue")` class. Then, to create a Gaussian mixture model, we select a mean $m_k$ from each class with probability $\pi_k = \frac{1}{10}$ and generate an observation from $N(m_k, I/5)$. We generate $100$ observations for each class using this method and train our model on the data.

```{r linearmodel, fig.cap="Classification example in two dimensions.", max_height = "50px", warning=FALSE}
# Fit simple linear model
lm_data <- data.frame(Y = as.numeric(test_mat$Y == "Orange"), x1 = test_mat$x1, x2 = test_mat$x2)
boundary_model <- lm(Y ~ x1 + x2, data = lm_data)

grid_predictions <- predict(boundary_model, grid_data)
grid_data$predicted_class <- ifelse(grid_predictions > 0.5, "Orange", "Blue")

# Create the plot with background
p <- ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = predicted_class), 
             size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_point(data = test_mat, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = Y), 
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8, stroke = 1, shape = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#4A90E2", "#F5A623")) +
  labs(title="Linear Regression") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust=0.5)
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.75)

# Add decision boundary line
boundary_slope <- -coef(boundary_model)[2] / coef(boundary_model)[3]
boundary_intercept <- (0.5 - coef(boundary_model)[1]) / coef(boundary_model)[3]

p + geom_abline(slope = boundary_slope, intercept = boundary_intercept, 
                color = "black", linewidth = 1)
```

In \@ref(fig:linearmodel), the set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ classified as `r color("ORANGE", "orange")` corresponds to $\{x:x^T\hat{\beta}\gt0.5\}$, and the two predicted classes are separated by the linear $\textit{decision boundary}\;\{x:x^T\hat{\beta} = 0.5\}$. There are some misclassifications in the simulated data.

### Nearest-Neighbor Methods
The nearest-neighborhood methods use observations in the training set $\mathcal{T}: \{(x_i, y_i),\;i=1,2,...,N\}$ closest in input space to $x$ to obtain $\hat{Y}$. We define the $k$-nearest neighbor fit for $\hat{Y}$ as

$$
\hat{Y}(x) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{x_i\in N_k(x)} y_i
$$

, where $N_k(x) = \{x_i \in \mathcal{T}:d(x_i,x) \leq d_{(k)}(x)\}$ is the $k$-nearest neighborhood of point $x$ and $|N_k| = k$. Here $d_{(k)}(x)$ denotes the $k$-th smallest distance from $x$ to the training points,  and $d(x_i,x)$ is the Euclidean norm $||x_i-x||_2$.[^6] The fitted values $\hat{Y}$ is converted to a class label $\hat{G}$ as in Equation \@ref(eq:classlabel).

```{r KNNexample, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", fig.cap="Example of a 3-NN algorithm."}
knitr::include_graphics("Chp2/pictures/note2_1.png")
```

We can further define $k$-NN more formally as:

Given test point $\textbf{x}$, we define the set of the $k$ nearest neighbors of the neighborhood $N_k$. $N_k$ is formally defined as $N_k \subseteq \mathcal{T}$ s.t. $|N_k| = k$. Furthermore, given $\forall(\textbf{x}', y') \in \mathcal{T}\backslash N_k$,[^7]

$$
d(\textbf{x}, \textbf{x}') \geq \operatorname*{argmax}_{(\textbf{x}'', y'') \in N_k} d(\textbf{x}, \textbf{x}'')
$$

We can now define a classifier $h$ as a function returning the most common label in $N_k$:

$$
h(\textbf{x}) = \text{mode}(\{y'': (x', y') \in N_k\})
$$
, where we classify the neighborhood by the label of the highest occurrence.

```{r knn1, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="The classes are fit by 15-nearest neighbor averaging. The predicted class is chosen by majority vote amongst lthe 15-nearest neighbor."}
train_features <- test_mat[, c("x1", "x2")]
test_data <- grid_data[, c("x1", "x2")]
train_labels <- test_mat$Y

# knn implementation 
knn_model <- knn(train = train_features, test = test_data, cl = train_labels, k=15)
grid_data$predicted_class <- knn_model
grid_data$pred_numeric <- as.numeric(knn_model == "Orange")

# Create the plot with background
ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = predicted_class), 
             size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_contour(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, z = pred_numeric), 
             breaks = 0.5, size = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = test_mat, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = Y), 
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8, stroke = 1, shape = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#4A90E2", "#F5A623")) +
  labs(title="15-Nearest Neighbor Classifier") + 
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.75)
```

In Figure \@ref(fig:knn1), the same training data is used as in Figure \@ref(fig:linearmodel), and we used 15-nearest-neighbor averaging of the binary coded response as the method of fitting. $\hat{Y}$ is the proportion of `r color("ORANGE", "orange")`'s in the neighborhood, and so assigning class `r color("ORANGE", "orange")` to $\hat{G}$ if $\hat{Y} \gt 0.5$ is essentially a majority vote in the neighborhood. We see that the decision boundaries are irregular, non-linear, and respond to local clusters where one class dominates.

```{r knn2, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Classes are coded as a binary variable and then predicted by 1-nearest neighbor classification."}
# knn implementation 
knn_model <- knn(train = train_features, test = grid_data[, c("x1", "x2")], cl = train_labels, k=1)
grid_data$predicted_class <- knn_model
grid_data$pred_numeric <- as.numeric(knn_model == "Orange")

# Create the plot with background
ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = predicted_class), 
             size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_contour(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, z = pred_numeric), 
             breaks = 0.5, size = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = test_mat, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = Y), 
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8, stroke = 1, shape = 1) +
  labs(title="1-Nearest Neighbor Classifier") + 
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#4A90E2", "#F5A623")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.75)
```

Figure \@ref(fig:knn2) instead shows the result for 1-nearest neighbor classification: $\hat{Y}$ is assigned the value $y_{\ell}$ of the closest point $x_{\ell}$ to $x$ in the training data. This corresponds to a Voronoi tessellation of the training data. Each point $x_i$ has an associated Voroni cell bounding the region where it is the only closest neighbor to any test point, such that, for all points $x$ in the cell, $\hat{G}(x) = g_i$. Compared to Figure \@ref(fig:knn1), the decision boundary is even more irregular. The training error is approximately an increasing function of $k$, and is always $0$ for $k=1$. As a consequence, we cannot use sum-of-squared errors on the training set as a criterion for picking $k$, as this would always result in picking $k=1$. 

It appears that $k$-nearest neighbor fits have a single parameter, which is the number of neighbors $k$, compared to the $p$ parameters in least-squares fit. However, the effective number of parameters of $k$-nn is $N/k$ and is generally bigger than $p$, which decreases with increasing $k$. Consider the hypothetical case where the neighborhoods are non-overlapping, given $N$ training points and $k$ points per neighborhood, we will have $N/k$ neighborhoods. As a majority vote will be casted for each neighborhood, this effectively constitutes a parameter for each neighborhood.

```{r misclassification, warning=FALSE, fig.cap = "Misclassification curves. A single training sample of size 200 was used, and a test sample of size 10000. Orange curve is test and blue curve is training error for k-nn classification. Results for the linear regression are the orange and blue squares at 3 degree of freedom. "}
# Training set
train_obs1 <- gen_obs(d1, 5000)
train_obs2 <- gen_obs(d2, 5000)
Y <- rep(c(0,1), each = 5000) # 0=BLUE, 1=ORANGE
train_mat <- as.data.frame(cbind(Y, rbind(train_obs1, train_obs2)))
train_mat$Y <- factor(train_mat$Y, labels = c("Blue", "Orange"))

# Test set
test_obs1 <- gen_obs(d1, 100)
test_obs2 <- gen_obs(d2, 100)
Y <- rep(c(0,1), each = 100)
test_mat <- as.data.frame(cbind(Y, rbind(test_obs1, test_obs2)))
test_mat$Y <- factor(test_mat$Y, labels = c("Blue", "Orange"))

# KNN model
k <- c(1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 17, 21, 25, 31, 39, 45, 55, 69, 89, 101, 125, 151)
results <- data.frame(k = numeric(), test_error = numeric(), train_error = numeric())

for (kval in k){
  test_pred <- knn(train = train_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], test = test_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], cl = train_mat$Y, k = kval)
  train_pred <- knn(train = train_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], test = train_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], cl = train_mat$Y, k = kval)
  
  test_error <- mean(test_pred != test_mat$Y)
  train_error <- mean(train_pred != train_mat$Y)
  
  results <- rbind(results, data.frame(k=kval, test_error=test_error, train_error=train_error))
}

results$df <- 200 / results$k

# Linear 
lm_data <- data.frame(Y = as.numeric(train_mat$Y == "Orange"), x1 = train_mat$x1, x2 = train_mat$x2)
lm_train <- lm(Y~x1+x2, data=lm_data)

test_pred <- ifelse(predict(lm_train, test_mat[,c("x1", "x2")]) > 0.5, "Orange", "Blue")
train_pred <- ifelse(predict(lm_train, train_mat[,c("x1", "x2")]) > 0.5, "Orange", "Blue")

lm_test_error <- mean(test_pred != test_mat$Y)
lm_train_error <- mean(train_pred != train_mat$Y)
# bayes_error <- 0.21

ggplot(results, aes(x = df)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = train_error, color = "Train"), linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(y = train_error, color = "Train"), size = 2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = test_error, color = "Test"), linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "solid") +
  geom_point(aes(y = test_error, color = "Test"), size = 2) +
 # geom_hline(aes(yintercept = bayes_error, color = "Bayes"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 3, y = lm_train_error, color = "Train"), shape = 15, size = 2.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 3, y = lm_test_error, color = "Test"), shape = 15, size = 2.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 3.5, y = lm_train_error + 0.01, label = "Linear", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Train" = "#4A90E2", "Test" = "#F5A623", "Bayes" = "#8B4789"),
                     breaks = c("Train", "Test", "Bayes")) +
  scale_x_log10(
    name = "Degrees of Freedom = N/k",
    breaks = c(2, 3, 5, 8, 12, 18, 29, 67, 200),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ 200/., name = "k = Number of Nearest Neighbors",
                        breaks = c(1, 3, 5, 7, 11, 21, 31, 45, 69, 101, 151))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Test Error", limits = c(0, 0.35), breaks = seq(0.05, 0.3, 0.05)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.15, 0.15),
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.title.x.top = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10)),
    axis.title.x.bottom = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  )

```

## Statistical Decision Theory 

We now develop a framework to develop and evaluate models that were previously discussed. Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^p$ denote a real valued input vector, and $Y \in \mathbb{R}$ a real-valued random output variable, with joint distribution $Pr(X,Y)$. We seek a function $f(X)$ for predicting $Y$ given input $X$. As we desire models that are accurate, we need a loss function $L(Y, f(X))$ that penalizes errors in prediction. The most common loss function is the squared-error loss: $L(Y, f(X)) = (Y - f(X))^2$. This gives us a criterion to select $f$

### Regression Function

Given the squared error loss function, we now define the expected prediction error criterion:

$$ 
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{EPE}(f) &= \text{E}[(Y-f(X))^2] \\
       &= \int (y-f(x))^2\text{Pr}(dx,dy)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

By conditioning on $X$, we can write \text{EPE} as:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{EPE}(f) &= \int_{y\in Y}\int_{x\in X} (y-f(x))^2\;\text{Pr}(X=x, Y=y)\;dx\;dy \\
              &= \int_{y\in Y}\int_{x\in X} (y-f(x))^2\;\text{Pr}(Y=y|\;X=x)\;\text{Pr}(X=x)\;dx\;dy \\ 
              &= \int_{x\in X}\int_{y\in Y} ((y-f(x))^2\;\text{Pr}(Y=y|\;X=x)\;dy)\ \;\text{Pr}(X=x)\;dx \\ 
              &= \text{E}_X\text{E}_{Y|X}[(Y - f(X))^2 | X]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

When analyzing the expectation in detail, we can notice that \text{EPE} is an integral of the form:

$$
\begin{equation}
EPE(f) = \int_{x\in X} E[(Y-f(x))^2|x]\; dx
\end{equation}
$$

Given that the integral is always non-negative and $f(x)$ can be chosen independently for each $x$, minimizing each integrand is equivalent to minimize the integral. Therefore, we solve the simpler problem instead: At each point $x$, we seek to find a constant $c$ that minimize $E_{Y|X}[(Y-c)^2|X=x]$. In other words, it suffices to minimize $\text{EPE}$ pointwise:

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) = \operatorname*{argmin}_c \text{E}_{Y|X}[(Y-c)^2|X=x]
\end{equation}
$$

To solve this optimization problem, we first expand the inner term:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
f(x) &= \operatorname*{argmin}_c \text{E}_{Y|X}[Y^2 + c^2 - 2Yc|X=x] \\
     &= \operatorname*{argmin}_c (\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y^2|X=x] + \text{E}_{Y|X}[c^2|X=x] -\text{E}_{Y|X}[2Yc|X=x]) \\
     &= \operatorname*{argmin}_c (\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y^2|X=x]) + c^2 - 2c\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y|X=x])
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

We take the derivative of the RHS w.r.t. $f(x) = c$ and set it to $0$:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
2c - 2\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y|X=x] &= 0 \\
c = \text{E}_{Y|X}[Y|X=x]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Hence, the solution to the optimization problem is the conditional expectation (regression function):

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) = \text{E}_{Y|X}[Y|X=x]
\end{equation}
$$

### Optimal Function of Nearest-Neighbor Methods and Linear Models



Assuming that the regression function $f(x)$ is approximately linear:

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) \approx x^{T}\beta
\end{equation}
$$
We plug this linear model into $\text{EPE}$,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{EPE}(f) &= \text{E}[(Y-f(X))^2] \\
              &= \text{E}[(Y-x^T\beta)^2] \\
              &= \text{E}[Y^2 + (x^T\beta)^2 -2x^TY\beta]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

We now differentiate in terms of $\beta$ (See the appendix for this chapter for the justification of the interchangeability of the derivative and integral sign), 

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\frac{\partial\text{EPE}(f)}{\partial \beta} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\int (Y^2 + (x^T\beta)^2 -2x^TY\beta)\;\text{Pr}(dx,dy) \\
                                             &= \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}(Y^2 + \beta^Txx^T\beta - 2x^TY\beta)\;\text{Pr}(dx,dy)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

By linearity of the integrals and derivatives,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\frac{\partial\text{EPE}(f)}{\partial \beta} &= \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}Y^2 + \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\beta^Txx^T\beta - \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}(2x^TY\beta) \\
                                             &= 0 + \int2xx^T\beta - \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}2Yx^T\beta \quad (\text{Using the identity:}\;\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\beta^Txx^T\beta = 2xx^T\beta)\\ 
                                             &= 2\beta\int xx^T - 2\int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta} Y\beta^Tx \quad \text{(Dot product is commutative)} \\
                                             &= 2\beta\text{E}[XX^T] - 2\text{E}[YX] \quad (\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\beta^Tx = \frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\sum\beta_ix_i = \sum x_i)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Setting $\frac{\partial\text{EPE}(f)}{\partial \beta} = 0$ to find the optimal value,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
2\beta\text{E}[XX^T] &= 2\text{E}[XY] \\
\beta &= (\text{E}[XX^T])^{-1}\text{E[XY]}
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$


[^1]: For a detailed proof of the derivation of the normal equation, see Appendix A at the end of this chapter.
[^2]: Proof for $\text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}) = \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T)$ can be accessed at this [Stack Exchange post](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/3573576/given-an-n-times-m-matrix-a-prove-that-\text{Col}ata-colat) 
[^3]: Derivation of the first-order characterization of a convex function can be accessed at this [pdf file](https://www.princeton.edu/~aaa/Public/Teaching/ORF523/S16/ORF523_S16_Lec7_gh.pdf)
[^4]: If $V$ is a nontrivial linear subspace, then there exists some nonzero vector $v\in V$. As $V$ is under scalar multiplication, $cv\in V$ for any scalar $c\in\mathbb{R}$. As $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable, it follows that $V$ has uncountable many elements 
[^5]: Essentially, the $p$-dim predicted surface generated by the statistical model is determined by the $p$ parameters. A simple linear regression of the form $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x_1$ forms a line, characterized by ($\beta_0$, $\beta_1$) where all possible values reside. On the other hand, a linear regression of two input variables $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x_1 + \beta_2x_2$ forms a plane, characterized by ($\beta_0$, $\beta_1$, $\beta_2$) where all possible values reside.
[^6]: Generally, the distance metric is specified by the Minkowski distance  
    $d(\textbf{x}, \textbf{z}) = (\sum_{r=1}^N |x_i - z_i|^p)^{\frac{1}{p}}$  
    Special cases include: $p = 1$ (Manhattan Distance), <br> $p = 2$ (Euclidean Distance), <br> $p \rightarrow \infty$ (Chebyshev Distance).
[^7]: Every point in $\mathcal{T}$ but not in $N_k$, is at least as far away from $\textbf{x}$ as the furthest point in $N_k$
